text.title: Focus Group Analysis: Themes, Cross-Group Comparison, and Reporting
text.summary: Post-session pipeline, cross-group comparison framework, theme extraction rules, and reporting structure.

section.title: Post-Session Pipeline

rule.statement: Use speaker diarization on all focus group recordings. Without speaker labels, transcript analysis cannot identify dominant-participant skew or per-segment quote attribution.
rule.rationale: Dominant participants can drive 40-60% of transcript volume. Without diarization, LLM theme extraction weights their views as group consensus. Diarization allows per-speaker filtering before analysis.

rule.statement: Run a dissent prompt pass explicitly after the consensus pass: "What disagreement existed within this group?" LLMs are biased toward consensus narratives and collapse nuanced dissent into a single theme without explicit prompting.
rule.rationale: Consensus-biased synthesis misses the finding that often matters most: the minority view, the segment-specific objection, the latent concern. Dissent prompting is the structural counter to this bias.

section.reference: Post-session pipeline: audio extract → diarize → theme extract

rule.statement: Tag transcripts with both speaker ID and segment label before analysis. Filter synthesis prompts by segment to surface cross-group contrasts: shared themes, segment-unique themes, and contradictions.
rule.rationale: Untagged cross-group analysis merges distinct segment signals and produces a false "global user" view. Segment filtering preserves the core value of running multiple groups.

rule.statement: Count unique speakers per theme, not utterance count, to assess theme strength. Polite agreement ("yeah, that's right") is not endorsement.
rule.rationale: High utterance count from a single speaker inflates theme strength artificially. Unique-speaker count is the correct denominator: consensus = most participants expressed the theme independently.

section.title: Reporting
section.p: Report structure per finding: theme name → what we heard (summary) → representative verbatim quote with speaker type → implication for design or strategy.

rule.statement: Report per-group findings before cross-group synthesis. Never aggregate groups before documenting group-level patterns.
rule.rationale: Early aggregation destroys segment signals. Cross-group synthesis must build on documented per-group patterns, not replace them.
